96.reason: [This loop only searches within the current line; multi-line comments spanning multiple lines are not handled correctly because the lexer processes each line independently without state.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 0.5 , reason:  This loop only searches within the current line; if the comment spans multiple lines, the closing "*/" on a later line is never found, causing the comment to be incorrectly terminated or subsequent characters to be misinterpreted.
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-2: 1.0 , reason:  [This loop only searches within the current line; multi-line comments spanning multiple lines are not handled correctly because the lexer processes each line independently without state.]

99.reason: The code fails to check if the closing '*/' exists before skipping it. If the comment is not closed within the current line (or file), i might go out of bounds or skip incorrectly. More importantly, for multi-line comments spanning multiple lines, this logic only handles single-line occurrences or fails to persist state across lines. However, the immediate bug causing the output difference is that the multi-line comment handling here assumes the comment ends on the same line. In the test case, the comment spans multiple lines. The lexer processes line by line. When it encounters /* on one line, it looks for */ on the *same* line. If not found, it likely breaks or behaves unexpectedly depending on loop termination. But looking at the buggy output, the content inside the comment `zhe shi ...` is being tokenized as identifiers. This means the multi-line comment detection failed to consume the comment content across lines. Since the lexer is called per line, it cannot handle multi-line comments that span across lines unless state is preserved. The current implementation resets `i` and `error` for every line. Thus, a multi-line comment starting on one line and ending on another will not be recognized as a comment on the subsequent lines, leading to the text inside being tokenized.

reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 0.5 , reason:  After the loop, if the comment did not close on this line, i may exceed the line length, and the remaining part of the comment on the next line will be scanned as normal code, potentially leading to lexical errors.

reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 1.0 , reason:  The code fails to check if the closing '*/' exists before skipping it. If the comment is not closed within the line, i might go out of bounds or skip incorrectly.

101.reason: [The loop may exit prematurely when the closing '*/' is at the very end of the line, causing i+1 to be out of bounds and skipping the final '*', leading to unterminated comment handling. More critically, if the multi-line comment spans multiple lines, this line-by-line tokenization approach cannot handle it correctly because each line is processed independently, so a '/* ... */' spanning two lines will not be recognized as a comment and the second line's content will be incorrectly lexed. However, in this specific test case, the issue is that the trailing "*/" appears at the end of the file inside a comment block, and after processing the comment, the lexer continues and processes the characters after the comment on the same line—but in the given input, there are no characters after "*/", yet the buggy output shows extra tokens "MULT *" and "DIV /", which correspond to a dangling "*/" being interpreted as '*' and '/' tokens. This happens because the multi-line comment logic fails to detect the closing "*/" properly when it appears at the very end of the input line, due to the condition `i + 1 < length` in the while loop guard preventing the check when i is at the last character. As a result, the loop exits without finding "*/", and the subsequent code treats the remaining '/' and '*' as individual operators.]

This happens because the multi-line comment logic fails to detect the closing "*/" properly when it appears at the very end of the input line, due to the condition `i + 1 < length` in the while loop guard preventing the check when i is at the last character. As a result, the loop exits without finding "*/", and the subsequent code treats the remaining '/' and '*' as individual operators.]
